title: @pure conflates referential transparency with no-allocation
description: @pure in ferrum-lang-ownership.md §2.4 has two stated behaviors: (1) it makes a correctness claim — same inputs produce same outputs (referential transparency), and (2) it suppresses ! Alloc from the visible effect signature. These are orthogonal properties. A function can be referentially transparent while allocating (e.g., fn intern(s: &str): &'static str that looks up or inserts into a static cache — same input always returns the same interned pointer, but allocates on first call). A function can be non-allocating but not referentially transparent (a pure math function). By conflating them, @pure means 'I claim this is pure AND I'm hiding its allocations'. Hiding allocation is a dangerous side effect to conflate with purity. Suggested fix: split into @pure (referential transparency only) and @hide_alloc or a separate mechanism for suppressing ! Alloc from the effect set. Or: document explicitly that @pure means 'treats internal allocation as an implementation detail of a pure interface', with clear no-alloc caller guidance.
close_reason: resolved by g91.1: @pure no longer suppresses ! Alloc; @no_alloc introduced as separate static-verification attribute. The conflation is gone.
